feedback.text: I have seen another citizen voicing these thoughts here before me and am mostly writing this to second their thoughts. Recently MasterCard/Visa have taken to using their elevated position to censor multiple markets. First Mangas¹ which they deemed inappropriate and now they've taken to doing the same in the video-game industry by threatening sellers like Steam. In my eyes banking companies like MasterCard and Visa shouldn't be able to write de-facto law by threatening trading blocks, if they deem a game or any other medium offensive/harmful they can go through the official channels to try and take it down. Now to a second issue I also feel the need to voice, is the shutting down of video-games by their publishers post the point of purchase. With how the law is currently written/enforced publishers can at will with none to very little warning shutdown their game and make it unplayable, even for those that bought it. I see that as a property rights violation because the customer has ownership over their copy of the game and the publisher violates that ownership by making their copy unusable post point of purchase. It happens for singleplayer games just like it happens for multiplayer games. There is, at the time of writing, a European Citizens Initiative fighting for the same cause, it has already amassed close to 1.5 million votes.
